date: 2026-06-14
title: Friedrich Nietzsche - Deep Dive

---

# Friedrich Nietzsche - Deep Dive

## Worldview Statements

| Statement | Source | Date | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| "There are no moral facts whatever. Moral judgement has this in common with religious judgement, that it believes in realities which do not exist." | Twilight of the Idols, "The 'Improvers' of Mankind" §1 | 1888 | Foundational anti-realism about morality |
| "Morality is merely an interpretation of certain phenomena, more precisely a misinterpretation." | Twilight of the Idols, "The 'Improvers' of Mankind" §1 | 1888 | Morality as hermeneutic error |
| "There are no moral phenomena at all, but only a moral interpretation of phenomena." | Beyond Good and Evil, §108 | 1886 | Core metaethical claim |
| "To speak of right and wrong per se makes no sense at all. No act of violence, rape, exploitation, destruction, is intrinsically 'unjust,' since life itself is violent, rapacious, exploitative, and destructive and cannot be conceived otherwise." | On the Genealogy of Morals, II §11 | 1887 | Life as will to power grounds amoralism |
| "What is good? Everything that heightens the feeling of power in man, the will to power, power itself. What is bad? Everything that is born of weakness." | The Antichrist, §2 | 1888 | Definitional replacement of Christian values |
| "What is happiness? The feeling that power is growing, that resistance is overcome." | The Antichrist, §2 | 1888 | Definition of happiness as power increase |
| "Not contentment but more power; not peace but war; not virtue but fitness." | The Antichrist, §2 | 1888 | Triad replacing Christian ideals |
| "The weak and the failures shall perish: first principle of our love of man. And they shall even be given every possible assistance." | The Antichrist, §2 | 1888 | Provocative inversion of Christian charity |
| "What is more harmful than any vice? Active pity for all the failures and all the weak: Christianity." | The Antichrist, §2 | 1888 | Pity as civilizational poison |
| "I regard Christianity as the most fatal seductive lie that has yet existed, as the great unholy lie." | Nachlass, KSA 13:15[10] | 1888 | Late notebook summation |
| "The Christian conception of God - God as God of the sick, God as spider, God as spirit - is one of the most corrupt conceptions of God arrived at on earth." | The Antichrist, §18 | 1888 | Attack on God-concept |
| "In Christianity neither morality nor religion has even a single point of contact with reality." | The Antichrist, §15 | 1888 | Christianity as total fantasy |
| "The concept of God invented as a counter-concept of life - everything harmful, poisonous, slanderous, the whole hostility unto death against life synthesized in this concept in a gruesome unity!" | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §8 | 1888 | God as anti-life |
| "The concept of the 'beyond,' the 'true world' invented in order to devaluate the only world there is - in order to retain no goal, no reason, no task for our earthly reality!" | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §8 | 1888 | Metaphysics as life-denial |
| "Buddhism is a hundred times more realistic than Christianity - it has the heritage of a cool and objective posing of problems in its composition." | The Antichrist, §20 | 1888 | Buddhism as superior to Christianity |
| "Buddhism is the only really positivistic religion history has to show us... it no longer speaks of 'the struggle against sin' but, quite in accordance with actuality, 'the struggle against suffering.'" | The Antichrist, §20 | 1888 | Buddhism's epistemic honesty |
| "Truth is the kind of error without which a certain species of living being could not live. The value for life is ultimately decisive." | Nachlass, KSA 11:34[253] | 1885 | Pragmatist-perspectival theory of truth |
| "There are no facts, only interpretations." | Nachlass, KSA 12:7[60] | 1886-87 | Radical perspectivism |
| "Against positivism, which halts at phenomena - 'There are only facts' - I would say: No, facts is precisely what there is not, only interpretations." | Nachlass, KSA 12:7[60] | 1886-87 | Full context of perspectivism |
| "Insofar as the word 'knowledge' has any meaning, the world is knowable; but it is interpretable otherwise, it has no meaning behind it, but countless meanings. - 'Perspectivism.'" | Nachlass, KSA 12:7[60] | 1886-87 | Perspectivism named |
| "We have art in order not to die of the truth." | Nachlass, KSA 13:16[40] | 1888 | Art as existential protection |
| "Art is worth more than truth." | Nachlass, KSA 12:9[60] | 1887 | Art over epistemology |
| "Art and nothing but art! It is the great means of making life possible, the great seduction to life, the great stimulant of life." | Nachlass, KSA 13:17[3] | 1888 | Art as life-affirmation |
| "Art as the single superior counterforce to all will to denial of life, as that which is anti-Christian, anti-Buddhist, anti-nihilist par excellence." | Nachlass, KSA 13:17[3] | 1888 | Art's ontological role |
| "Physiologically too, science rests on the same foundation as the ascetic ideal: a certain impoverishment of life is the presupposition of both." | On the Genealogy of Morals, III §25 | 1887 | Science as disguised asceticism |
| "It is not the victory of science that distinguishes our nineteenth century, but the victory of scientific method over science." | Nachlass, KSA 12:15[51] | 1887 | Method over content |
| "To live alone one must be a beast or a god, says Aristotle. Leaving out the third case: one must be both - a philosopher." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §3 | 1888 | Philosopher as hybrid being |
| "Formula of my happiness: a Yes, a No, a straight line, a goal." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §44 | 1888 | Personal credo of directness |
| "Without music, life would be a mistake." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §33 | 1888 | Music as existential necessity |
| "Is man merely a mistake of God's? Or God merely a mistake of man's?" | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §7 | 1888 | Inversion of theological question |
| "What does not kill me makes me stronger." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §8 | 1888 | Suffering as strength |
| "The spiritualization of sensuality is called love: it represents a great triumph over Christianity." | Twilight of the Idols, "Morality as Anti-Nature" §3 | 1888 | Love as anti-Christian victory |
| "All truly great thoughts are conceived while walking." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §34 | 1888 | Walking as philosophical method |
| "Sit as little as possible; give no credence to any thought that was not born outdoors and of free movement - in which the muscles are not also revelling." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §1 | 1888 | Embodied thinking |
| "Only thoughts reached by walking have value." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §34 | 1888 | Restatement on walking |
| "This is my way; where is yours? - thus I answered those who asked me 'the way.' For the way - that does not exist." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Spirit of Gravity" §2 | 1884 | Anti-universalism of paths |
| "I am a law only for my kind, I am no law for all." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Higher Man" §4 | 1885 | Aristocratic particularism |
| "One must learn to love oneself - thus do I teach - with a wholesome and healthy love: so that one can bear to be with oneself and need not roam." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Spirit of Gravity" §2 | 1884 | Self-love as foundation |
| "The higher its type, the more rarely does a thing succeed." | Beyond Good and Evil, §62 | 1886 | Rarity of excellence |
| "Every elevation of the type 'man' has hitherto been the work of an aristocratic society." | Beyond Good and Evil, §257 | 1886 | Aristocracy and human enhancement |
| "Life itself is essentially appropriation, injury, overpowering of the strange and weaker, suppression, severity, imposition of one's own forms, incorporation and, at the least and mildest, exploitation." | Beyond Good and Evil, §259 | 1886 | Will to power as life's essence |
| "Exploitation does not belong to a depraved or imperfect or primitive society: it belongs to the nature of the living being as a primary organic function." | Beyond Good and Evil, §259 | 1886 | Exploitation naturalized |
| "A living thing seeks above all to discharge its strength - life itself is will to power." | Beyond Good and Evil, §13 | 1886 | Will to power thesis |
| "Supposing that nothing else is 'given' as real but our world of desires and passions... would it not be permitted to make the experiment and to ask the question whether this which is given does not suffice for an understanding even of the so-called mechanical (or 'material') world?" | Beyond Good and Evil, §36 | 1886 | Will to power as cosmological hypothesis |
| "My idea is that every specific body strives to become master of all space and to extend its force (its will to power) and to thrust back all that resists its extension." | Nachlass, KSA 13:14[186] | 1888 | Will to power at cosmic scale |
| "This world is the will to power - and nothing besides! And you yourselves are also this will to power - and nothing besides!" | Nachlass, KSA 11:38[12] | 1885 | Monistic will to power |
| "What is the greatest thing ye can experience? It is the hour of great contempt. The hour in which even your happiness becometh loathsome unto you, and so also your reason and virtue." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §3 | 1883 | Contempt as catalyst |
| "Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman - a rope over an abyss." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §4 | 1883 | Humanity as transition |
| "What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not an end." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §4 | 1883 | Anti-humanism |
| "The thought of suicide is a great consolation: by means of it one gets through many a bad night." | Beyond Good and Evil, §157 | 1886 | Dark pragmatism |
| "I obviously do everything to be 'hard to understand' myself - and one should be heartily grateful for the good will to some subtlety of interpretation." | Beyond Good and Evil, §27 | 1886 | Deliberate difficulty |
| "The essential thing 'in heaven and upon earth' seems, to say it once more, to be a protracted obedience in one direction: from out of that there always results and has always resulted in the long run something for the sake of which it is worthwhile to live on earth." | Beyond Good and Evil, §188 | 1886 | Discipline as purpose |
| "Every philosophy is the philosophy of some stage of life." | Human, All Too Human, Preface §6 | 1886 | Philosophy as autobiography |
| "Gradually it has become clear to me what every great philosophy so far has been: namely, the personal confession of its author and a kind of involuntary and unconscious memoir." | Beyond Good and Evil, §6 | 1886 | Philosophy as masked confession |
| "The fundamental faith of the metaphysicians is the faith in antithetical values." | Beyond Good and Evil, §2 | 1886 | Against binary thinking |
| "The falseness of a judgement is for us not necessarily an objection to a judgement... The question is to what extent it is life-promoting, life-preserving, species-preserving, perhaps even species-cultivating." | Beyond Good and Evil, §4 | 1886 | Pragmatic epistemology |
| "To recognize untruth as a condition of life: that, to be sure, means to resist customary value-sentiments in a dangerous fashion; and a philosophy which ventures to do so places itself, by that act alone, beyond good and evil." | Beyond Good and Evil, §4 | 1886 | Untruth as vital |
| "I mistrust all systematizers and I avoid them. The will to a system is a lack of integrity." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §26 | 1888 | Anti-systematicity |
| "Convictions are more dangerous enemies of truth than lies." | Human, All Too Human, §483 | 1878 | Against certainty |
| "The individual has always had to struggle to keep from being overwhelmed by the tribe. If you try it, you will be lonely often, and sometimes frightened. But no price is too high to pay for the privilege of owning yourself." | Attributed, possibly from Schopenhauer as Educator | c. 1874 | Individualism over conformity |
| "One repays a teacher badly if one always remains nothing but a pupil." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Bestowing Virtue" §3 | 1883 | Anti-discipleship |
| "The surest way to corrupt a youth is to instruct him to hold in higher esteem those who think alike than those who think differently." | The Dawn, §297 | 1881 | Education against conformity |
| "Thoughts are the shadows of our feelings - always darker, emptier, simpler." | The Gay Science, §179 | 1882 | Primacy of affect |
| "I fear we are not getting rid of God because we still believe in grammar." | Twilight of the Idols, "'Reason' in Philosophy" §5 | 1888 | Language as theology |
| "We cease to think when we refuse to do so under the constraint of language." | Nachlass, KSA 12:5[22] | 1886 | Language constrains thought |
| "The demand to be loved is the greatest of all arrogant presumptions." | Nachlass, KSA 9:6[145] | 1880 | Against the claim to love |
| "Whoever despises himself still respects himself as one who despises." | Beyond Good and Evil, §78 | 1886 | Self-contempt as paradoxical self-regard |
| "One has to repay good and ill - but why precisely to the person who has done us good or ill?" | Beyond Good and Evil, §73 | 1886 | Against personal reciprocity |
| "There is a certain right to stupidity and a great advantage in being thought stupid - for then others let one be, which is the chief thing." | Nachlass, KSA 13:15[118] | 1888 | Solitude through underestimation |
| "The lonely one offers his hand too quickly to whomever he encounters." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Tree on the Mountain" | 1883 | Self-diagnosis of loneliness |
| "Shared joys, not shared sufferings, make a friend." | Human, All Too Human, §499 | 1878 | Friendship through strength |
| "In the end one loves one's desire and not what is desired." | Beyond Good and Evil, §175 | 1886 | Desire's self-referentiality |
| "Not that you lied to me but that I no longer believe you has shaken me." | Beyond Good and Evil, §183 | 1886 | Trust over truth |
| "Madness is rare in individuals - but in groups, parties, nations, and ages it is the rule." | Beyond Good and Evil, §156 | 1886 | Collective insanity |
| "Under peaceful conditions the warlike man attacks himself." | Beyond Good and Evil, §76 | 1886 | Self-directed aggression |
| "In revenge and in love woman is more barbarous than man." | Beyond Good and Evil, §139 | 1886 | On woman's intensity |
| "Woman's love involves injustice and blindness against everything that she does not love." | The Gay Science, §363 | 1882 | On woman's exclusivity |
| "Woman learns to hate in proportion as she unlearns to charm." | Beyond Good and Evil, §84 | 1886 | On feminine resentment |
| "When a woman has scholarly inclinations there is usually something wrong with her sexuality." | Beyond Good and Evil, §144 | 1886 | Provocative claim on women and intellect |
| "Everything about woman is a riddle, and everything about woman has one solution: that is pregnancy." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Little Old and Young Women" | 1883 | Essentialist claim |
| "The true man wants two things: danger and play. For that reason he wants woman, as the most dangerous plaything." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Little Old and Young Women" | 1883 | Man-woman dynamic |
| "You are going to women? Do not forget the whip!" | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Little Old and Young Women" | 1883 | Spoken by an old woman to Zarathustra |
| "The happiness of man is: I will. The happiness of woman is: he wills." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Little Old and Young Women" | 1883 | Gendered will |
| "Amor fati: let that be my love henceforth! I do not want to wage war against what is ugly. I do not want to accuse; I do not even want to accuse those who accuse. Looking away shall be my only negation." | The Gay Science, §276 | 1882 | Amor fati first stated |
| "My formula for greatness in a human being is amor fati: that one wants nothing to be different, not forward, not backward, not in all eternity. Not merely bear what is necessary, still less conceal it... but love it." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §10 | 1888 | Definitive amor fati |
| "I want to learn more and more to see as beautiful what is necessary in things; then I shall be one of those who make things beautiful." | The Gay Science, §276 | 1882 | Aesthetic transfiguration of necessity |
| "What if a demon were to creep after you one day or night, in your loneliest loneliness, and say: 'This life, as you now live it and have lived it, you will have to live once more and innumerable times more'... Would you not throw yourself down and gnash your teeth and curse the demon who spoke thus? Or have you once experienced a tremendous moment when you would have answered him: 'You are a god, and never have I heard anything more divine.'" | The Gay Science, §341 | 1882 | Eternal recurrence as existential test |
| "The greatest weight. - What, if some day or night a demon were to steal after you into your loneliest loneliness..." | The Gay Science, §341 | 1882 | Opening of the eternal recurrence passage |
| "Let us impress the image of eternity upon our lives! This thought contains more than all the religions which despise this life as a fleeting thing." | Nachlass, KSA 9:11[159] | 1881 | Eternal recurrence as religion-replacement |
| "My doctrine says: Live so that thou must desire to live again - that is thy duty - for in any case thou wilt live again!" | Nachlass, KSA 9:11[163] | 1881 | Recurrence as imperative |
| "We who are homeless. - Among Europeans today there is no lack of those who are entitled to call themselves homeless in a distinctive and honorable sense." | The Gay Science, §377 | 1887 (Book V) | Good European identity |
| "We are not nearly 'German' enough, in the sense in which the word 'German' is constantly being used nowadays, to advocate nationalism and race hatred." | The Gay Science, §377 | 1887 (Book V) | Anti-nationalism |
| "We who are homeless are too manifold and mixed racially and in our descent, being 'modern men,' and consequently do not feel tempted to participate in the mendacious racial self-admiration and racial indecency that parades in Germany today." | The Gay Science, §377 | 1887 (Book V) | Against racial pride |
| "I am a pure-blooded Polish nobleman, without a single drop of bad blood, certainly not German blood." | Letter to Georg Brandes | April 10, 1888 | Self-mythologizing Polish identity |
| "Deutschland, Deutschland uber alles - I fear that was the end of German philosophy." | Twilight of the Idols, "What the Germans Lack" §1 | 1888 | German nationalism kills thought |
| "The Germans - once they were called the nation of thinkers: do they still think at all? The Germans are now bored with intellect, the Germans mistrust intellect." | Twilight of the Idols, "What the Germans Lack" §1 | 1888 | German intellectual decline |
| "Culture and the state - one should not deceive oneself about this - are antagonists: 'Kultur-Staat' is merely a modern idea. One lives off the other, one thrives at the expense of the other." | Twilight of the Idols, "What the Germans Lack" §4 | 1888 | State versus culture |
| "The price we pay for the foundation of the Reich is enormous. Bismarck has destroyed the intellectual life of Germany." | Letter to Franz Overbeck | 1887 | Anti-Bismarck |
| "The Germans think they are strong when they have simply reassembled themselves in an overwhelming herd." | Nachlass, KSA 12:10[26] | 1887 | Germans as herd |
| "I have no word, I have only a look, for those who dare to speak the word 'Fatherland' in my presence." | Nachlass, KSA 13:25[6] | 1888-89 | Patriotism contempt |
| "Liberal institutions immediately cease to be liberal as soon as they are attained: subsequently there is nothing more thoroughly harmful to freedom than liberal institutions." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §38 | 1888 | Against institutional liberalism |
| "The democratic movement is the heir of the Christian movement." | Beyond Good and Evil, §202 | 1886 | Democracy as secularized Christianity |
| "The overall degeneration of man, right down to what today appears to the socialist dolts and flatheads as their 'man of the future' - as their ideal! - this degeneration and diminution of man to the perfect herd animal." | Beyond Good and Evil, §203 | 1886 | Anti-democratic elite claim |
| "Kant's joke. - Kant wanted to prove, in a way that would dumbfound the common man, that the common man was right: that was the secret joke of his soul." | The Gay Science, §193 | 1882 | Anti-Kant barb |
| "I have on the whole more faith in the instinct of an ass than in the 'reason' of a Kantian." | Nachlass, KSA 13:14[116] | 1888 | Contempt for Kantian reason |
| "Being deep and seeming deep. Whoever knows he is deep strives for clarity; whoever would like to seem deep to the crowd strives for obscurity." | The Gay Science, §173 | 1882 | Depth demands clarity |
| "Those who know that they are deep strive for clarity. Those who would like to seem deep to the crowd strive for obscurity." | The Gay Science, §173 | 1882 | Same, alternate translation |
| "It is my ambition to say in ten sentences what everyone else says in a whole book - what everyone else does not say in a whole book." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §51 | 1888 | Stylistic ambition |
| "Of all that is written I love only what a man has written with his blood. Write with blood, and you will find that blood is spirit." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Reading and Writing" | 1883 | Writing as embodiment |
| "A good aphorism is too hard for the tooth of time and is not consumed by all millennia, although it serves as food for every age: thus it is the great paradox of literature, the intransitory amid the changing." | Nachlass, KSA 8:23[55] | 1876-77 | Aphorism as eternal form |
| "A book ought to be an ice-axe to break the frozen sea within us." | Letter attribution disputed (likely Kafka) | - | Often misattributed |
| "The great style arises when the beautiful wins a victory over the monstrous." | Nachlass, KSA 12:10[167] | 1887 | Aesthetic theory of form over chaos |
| "Existence and the world are eternally justified only as aesthetic phenomena." | The Birth of Tragedy, §5 | 1872 | Aesthetic justification of existence |
| "Only as an aesthetic product can the world be justified to all eternity." | The Birth of Tragedy, §5 | 1872 | Core of early metaphysics |
| "We possess art lest we perish of the truth." | Nachlass, KSA 13:16[40] | 1888 | Art against nihilism |
| "Whoever has looked deeply into the world may well guess how much wisdom lies in the superficiality of men. The instinct that preserves them teaches them to be fickle, light, and false." | Beyond Good and Evil, §59 | 1886 | Surface as survival |
| "One should part from life as Odysseus parted from Nausicaa - blessing it rather than in love with it." | Human, All Too Human, II §408 | 1879 | Death with gratitude |
| "I know my fate. One day my name will be associated with the memory of something tremendous - a crisis without equal on earth." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §1 | 1888 | Prophetic self-assessment |
| "I am not a man, I am dynamite." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §1 | 1888 | Self as explosive |
| "I contradict as has never been contradicted before and am nevertheless the opposite of a nay-saying spirit." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §1 | 1888 | Negation in service of affirmation |
| "I was never yet modest enough to demand less of myself than I do." | Nachlass, KSA 10:3[1] | 1882 | Self-demand |
| "One must be able to lose oneself occasionally if one wants to learn something from things different from oneself." | Nachlass, KSA 9:6[344] | 1880 | Losing oneself for knowledge |
| "The most common form of human stupidity is forgetting what one is trying to do." | Nachlass (attributed) | undated | Against aimlessness |
| "Not to be a burden to others and yet to show kindness to those who would make us one." | Letter to Peter Gast | 1882 | Personal ethics of burden |
| "The doer alone learns." | Nachlass, KSA 10:12[22] | 1883 | Pragmatic epistemology |
| "One must still have chaos in oneself to be able to give birth to a dancing star." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §5 | 1883 | Chaos as creative condition |
| "I love those who do not know how to live except by going under, for they are those who cross over." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §4 | 1883 | Love of self-overcomers |
| "The earth has a skin and that skin has diseases; one of its diseases is called man." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Great Events" | 1884 | Anti-humanist provocation |
| "There is more reason in your body than in your best wisdom." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Despisers of the Body" | 1883 | Body over intellect |
| "Body am I entirely, and nothing else; and soul is only a word for something about the body." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Despisers of the Body" | 1883 | Anti-dualism |
| "Behind your thoughts and feelings, my brother, there stands a mighty ruler, an unknown sage - whose name is self. In your body he dwells; he is your body." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Despisers of the Body" | 1883 | Self as body |
| "The will to overcome an emotion is ultimately only the will of another, or of several other, emotions." | Beyond Good and Evil, §117 | 1886 | Emotions override emotions |
| "There is no such thing as willing, but only willing something: one must not remove the aim from the total condition." | Nachlass, KSA 12:1[62] | 1885-86 | Against abstract will |
| "All psychology so far has got stuck in moral prejudices and fears; it has not dared to descend into the depths." | Beyond Good and Evil, §23 | 1886 | Psychology as queen of sciences |
| "Psychology shall be recognized again as the queen of the sciences, for whose service and preparation the other sciences exist." | Beyond Good and Evil, §23 | 1886 | Psychology crowned |
| "The degree and nature of a man's sensuality extends to the highest altitudes of his spirit." | Beyond Good and Evil, §75 | 1886 | Sensuality and spirit linked |
| "Everything done out of love takes place beyond good and evil." | Beyond Good and Evil, §153 | 1886 | Love beyond morality |
| "Blessed are the forgetful: for they 'get the better' even of their blunders." | Beyond Good and Evil, §217 | 1886 | Forgetting as power |
| "The secret of the greatest fruitfulness and the greatest enjoyment of existence is: to live dangerously!" | The Gay Science, §283 | 1882 | Danger as vitality |
| "For believe me: the secret for harvesting from existence the greatest fruitfulness and the greatest enjoyment is - to live dangerously! Build your cities on the slopes of Vesuvius!" | The Gay Science, §283 | 1882 | Full passage on danger |
| "I say unto you: one must still have chaos in oneself to be able to give birth to a dancing star. I say unto you: you still have chaos in yourselves." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §5 | 1883 | Chaos and creation |
| "Of all the animals on earth, man has the most cruelty." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Tarantulas" | 1884 | Human cruelty |
| "Wherever I found the living, there I found will to power; and even in the will of those who serve I found the will to be master." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Self-Overcoming" | 1884 | Will to power universalized |
| "And life itself confided this secret to me: 'Behold,' it said, 'I am that which must always overcome itself.'" | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Self-Overcoming" | 1884 | Life as self-overcoming |
| "Where I found a living thing, there I found the will to power; and even in the will of the servant I found the will to be master." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Self-Overcoming" | 1884 | Alternative translation |
| "What does your conscience say? - 'You shall become the person you are.'" | The Gay Science, §270 | 1882 | Become who you are |
| "How one becomes what one is." | Ecce Homo, subtitle | 1888 | Self-becoming |
| "I want to learn more and more how to see what is necessary in things as what is beautiful in them - thus I will be one of those who make things beautiful." | The Gay Science, §276 | 1882 | Beautifying necessity |
| "One thing is needful. - To 'give style' to one's character - a great and rare art! It is practised by those who survey all the strengths and weaknesses of their nature and then fit them into an artistic plan." | The Gay Science, §290 | 1882 | Self-styling |
| "I love him who is ashamed when the dice fall in his favor, and who then asks: 'Am I a dishonest player?' - for he wants to perish." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §4 | 1883 | Love of honest gamblers |
| "Do I still have a goal? A haven towards which my sail is set? A good wind? Ah, only he who knows whither he sails knows what wind is good and a fair wind for him." | The Gay Science, §382 | 1887 | Directional existence |
| "We have left the land and have embarked. We have burned our bridges behind us - indeed, we have gone further and destroyed the land behind us." | The Gay Science, §124 | 1882 | No return possible |
| "Woe to him who has no home!" | The Gay Science, §377 | 1887 | The homeless condition |
| "I love the great despisers because they are the great adorers and arrows of longing for the other shore." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §4 | 1883 | Contempt as reversed love |
| "The most spiritual men, as the strongest, find their happiness where others would find their destruction." | The Antichrist, §57 | 1888 | Spiritual strength |
| "He who has a why to live for can bear almost any how." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §12 | 1888 | Purpose over comfort |
| "In the mountains of truth you never climb in vain: either you will get up higher today, or you will exercise your powers so that you can climb higher tomorrow." | Human, All Too Human, §358 | 1878 | Effort never wasted |
| "The maturity of man - that means, to have reacquired the seriousness that one had as a child at play." | Beyond Good and Evil, §94 | 1886 | Play as seriousness |
| "A thinker sees his own actions as experiments and questions - as attempts to find out something. Success and failure are for him answers above all." | The Gay Science, §41 | 1882 | Life as experiment |
| "We should consider every day lost on which we have not danced at least once." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra (attributed) | 1883-85 | Dance as obligation |
| "Our vanity is hardest to wound precisely when our pride has just been wounded." | Beyond Good and Evil, §170 | 1886 | Vanity-pride dynamic |
| "That which does not kill us makes us stronger." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §8 | 1888 | Alternate translation |
| "The snake which cannot cast its skin has to die. As well the minds which are prevented from changing their opinions; they cease to be mind." | The Dawn, §573 | 1881 | Change as life |
| "A very popular error: having the courage of one's convictions; rather it is a matter of having the courage for an attack on one's convictions!" | Nachlass, KSA 13:16[32] | 1888 | Self-attack |
| "Man does not strive for happiness; only the Englishman does that." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §12 | 1888 | Anti-utilitarian barb |
| "I fear that the animals consider man as a being like themselves that has lost in a most dangerous way its sound animal common sense; they consider him the insane animal, the laughing animal, the weeping animal, the unhappy animal." | The Gay Science, §224 | 1882 | Human as failed animal |
| "Every profound spirit needs a mask: even more, around every profound spirit a mask is continually growing." | Beyond Good and Evil, §40 | 1886 | Masks as necessity |
| "To be ashamed of one's immorality: that is a step on the ladder at the end of which one is ashamed also of one's morality." | Beyond Good and Evil, §95 | 1886 | Beyond moral shame |
| "He who would learn to fly one day must first learn to stand and walk and run and climb and dance; one cannot fly into flying." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Spirit of Gravity" | 1884 | Gradual ascent |
| "The most common lie is that which one lies to himself; lying to others is relatively an exception." | The Antichrist, §55 | 1888 | Self-deception as norm |
| "The thought of death. It gives me a melancholy happiness to live in the midst of this confusion of streets, of necessities, of voices: how much enjoyment, impatience, and desire, how much thirsty life and drunkenness of life comes to light here every moment! And yet it will soon be so still for all these shouting, lively, life-loving people!" | Human, All Too Human, §322 | 1878 | Mortality awareness |
| "One must be a sea, to receive a polluted stream without becoming impure." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Bestowing Virtue" | 1883 | Capacity to absorb negativity |
| "The noble soul has reverence for itself." | Beyond Good and Evil, §287 | 1886 | Noble self-reverence |
| "What is noble? ... That one instinctively seeks heavy responsibilities." | Nachlass, KSA 12:10[45] | 1887 | Nobility as burden-seeking |
| "To him who feels himself preordained to contemplation and not to belief, all believers are too noisy and obtrusive; he guards against them." | The Gay Science, §366 | 1887 | Contemplation over belief |
| "I assessed the value of men, of races, according to how necessarily they could not understand the god apart from the satyr." | Ecce Homo, "The Birth of Tragedy" §2 | 1888 | Dionysian criterion of value |
| "The Dionysian man resembles Hamlet: both have once looked truly into the essence of things, they have gained knowledge, and nausea inhibits action." | The Birth of Tragedy, §7 | 1872 | Knowledge and nausea |
| "Excess strength alone is proof of strength." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §16 | 1888 | Overflow as evidence |
| "We are unknown to ourselves, we men of knowledge - and with good reason. We have never sought ourselves - how could it happen that we should ever find ourselves?" | On the Genealogy of Morals, Preface §1 | 1887 | Self-ignorance of scholars |
| "Genuine honesty, assuming it is our virtue and we cannot get rid of it, we free spirits - well then, we will want to work on it with all our malice and love." | Beyond Good and Evil, §227 | 1886 | Honesty as free-spirit virtue |
| "Independence is for the very few; it is a privilege of the strong." | Beyond Good and Evil, §29 | 1886 | Independence as privilege |
| "Anyone who has looked deeply into the world may guess how much wisdom lies in the superficiality of men." | Beyond Good and Evil, §59 | 1886 | Praise of surfaces |
| "What a man is begins to betray itself when his talent decreases - when he ceases to show what he can do." | Beyond Good and Evil, §130 | 1886 | Character revealed in decline |
| "People who comprehend a thing to its very depths rarely stay faithful to it forever." | Human, All Too Human, §72 | 1878 | Understanding breeds distance |
| "We love life, not because we are used to living but because we are used to loving." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Reading and Writing" | 1883 | Love over habit |
| "It is not a lack of love, but a lack of friendship that makes unhappy marriages." | Human, All Too Human, §406 | 1878 | Marriage needs friendship |
| "He who despises himself, nevertheless esteems himself thereby, as a despiser." | Human, All Too Human, §78 | 1878 | Self-contempt as self-regard |
| "All things are subject to interpretation. Whichever interpretation prevails at a given time is a function of power and not truth." | Nachlass (attributed) | undated | Power determines interpretation |
| "There is always some madness in love. But there is also always some reason in madness." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Reading and Writing" | 1883 | Love-madness dialectic |
| "I have done that, says my memory. I cannot have done that, says my pride, and remains inexorable. Eventually - memory yields." | Beyond Good and Evil, §68 | 1886 | Pride over memory |
| "Ultimately, it is the desire, not the desired, that we love." | Beyond Good and Evil, §175 | 1886 | Desire loves itself |
| "In individuals, insanity is rare; but in groups, parties, nations and epochs, it is the rule." | Beyond Good and Evil, §156 | 1886 | Collective madness |
| "The devotion of the greatest is to encounter risk and danger, and play dice for death." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On War and Warriors" | 1883 | Risk as devotion |
| "Distrust all in whom the impulse to punish is powerful." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Adder's Bite" | 1884 | Against punishers |
| "The earth has a skin; and this skin has diseases. One of these diseases, for example, is called 'man.'" | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Great Events" | 1884 | Misanthropic provocation |
| "Man would rather will nothingness than not will." | On the Genealogy of Morals, III §28 | 1887 | Final thesis of the Genealogy |
| "The ascetic ideal means this: that something was lacking, that man was surrounded by a fearful void - he needed a meaning... The ascetic ideal offered him a meaning." | On the Genealogy of Morals, III §28 | 1887 | Meaning over nihilism |
| "Rather than want nothing, man even wants nothingness." | On the Genealogy of Morals, III §28 | 1887 | Alternate formulation |
| "The will would rather will nothingness than not will. - But I shall say it once more: this very nothingness was still a will." | On the Genealogy of Morals, III §28 | 1887 | Closing of the Genealogy |
| "What I understand by 'philosopher': a terrible explosive in the presence of which everything is in danger." | Ecce Homo, "The Untimely Ones" §3 | 1888 | Philosopher as danger |
| "The discipline of suffering, of great suffering - do you not know that only this discipline has created all enhancements of man so far?" | Beyond Good and Evil, §225 | 1886 | Suffering creates greatness |
| "That tension of the soul in unhappiness which cultivates its strength, its shudders face to face with great ruin, its inventiveness and courage in enduring, persevering, interpreting, and exploiting suffering." | Beyond Good and Evil, §225 | 1886 | Soul-tension through suffering |
| "The thought of suicide is a great comfort: it helps one through many a dreadful night." | Beyond Good and Evil, §157 | 1886 | Suicide as consolation |
| "No shepherd and one herd! Everybody wants the same, everybody is the same: whoever feels different goes voluntarily into a madhouse." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §5 | 1883 | Last man critique |
| "'We have invented happiness,' say the last men, and they blink." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §5 | 1883 | Contempt for comfort |
| "The most spiritual human beings, assuming they are the most courageous, also experience by far the most painful tragedies: but it is precisely for this reason that they honor life, because it brings against them its most formidable weapons." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §17 | 1888 | Tragedy honors life |
| "One has to pay dearly for immortality; one has to die several times while one is still alive." | Ecce Homo, "Thus Spoke Zarathustra" §5 | 1888 | Cost of greatness |
| "The thought of eternal recurrence: what I demand, namely the courage to accept or the confession that one has not the courage." | Nachlass, KSA 11:25[290] | 1884 | Recurrence as courage-test |
| "My suffering and my compassion for suffering - what does it matter! Am I concerned with happiness? I am concerned with my work!" | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "The Honey Sacrifice" | 1885 | Work over happiness |
| "Weariness that wants to reach the ultimate with one leap, with one fatal leap, a poor ignorant weariness that does not want to want anymore: this created all gods and afterworlds." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Afterworldly" | 1883 | Religion from exhaustion |
| "Not by wrath does one kill, but by laughter. Come, let us kill the Spirit of Gravity!" | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Reading and Writing" | 1883 | Laughter as weapon |
| "I would believe only in a god who could dance." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Reading and Writing" | 1883 | Dancing god |
| "Whoever fights monsters should see to it that in the process he does not become a monster. And if you gaze long enough into an abyss, the abyss will gaze back into you." | Beyond Good and Evil, §146 | 1886 | Moral contamination warning |
| "There is a false saying: 'How can someone who can't save himself save others?' But if I have the key to your chains, why should your lock and my lock be the same?" | The Dawn, §325 | 1881 | Against self-help prerequisite |
| "The great epochs of our life are at the points when we gain courage to rebaptize our badness as the best in us." | Beyond Good and Evil, §116 | 1886 | Transvaluation of personal evil |
| "The most perfidious way of harming a cause consists of defending it deliberately with faulty arguments." | The Gay Science, §191 | 1882 | Sabotage through bad defense |
| "What is originality? To see something that has no name as yet and hence cannot be mentioned although it stares us all in the face." | The Gay Science, §261 | 1882 | Originality as naming |
| "It is nobler to declare oneself wrong than to insist on being right - especially when one is right." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Higher Man" §7 | 1885 | Noble concession |
| "There are cases in which we are like horses, we psychologists, and become restless: we see our own shadow wavering up and down before us. A psychologist must turn his eyes from himself to eye anything at all." | Nachlass (attributed) | undated | Psychologist's self-blindness |
| "The sick are the greatest danger for the well... It is not the strongest but the weakest who undermine life among men." | On the Genealogy of Morals, III §14 | 1887 | Weakness as social danger |
| "What do you consider the most humane? To spare someone shame." | The Gay Science, §274 | 1882 | Humane as shame-sparing |
| "We fly to the beauty of form as a relief from the dreadful pressure of the content of existence." | Nachlass (early) | early 1870s | Form as relief |
| "The most vulnerable and yet most unconquerable thing is human vanity: nay its strength increases and can in the end become gigantic, through being wounded." | Nachlass (early Basler notebooks) | 1870s | Vanity strengthened by wounds |
| "Great indebtedness does not make men grateful, but vengeful; and if a little charity is not forgotten, it turns into a gnawing worm." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Pitying" | 1884 | Charity breeds resentment |
| "When we are tired, we are attacked by ideas we conquered long ago." | Nachlass (attributed) | undated | Fatigue and regression |
| "That for which we find words is something already dead in our hearts. There is always a kind of contempt in the act of speaking." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §26 | 1888 | Language as mortification |
| "A pair of powerful spectacles has sometimes sufficed to cure a person in love; and whoever had the imagination to represent a face or a body twenty years older, would perhaps go through life with a very undisturbed heart." | Human, All Too Human, §409 | 1878 | Love cured by imagination |
| "The higher man is distinguished from the lower by his fearlessness and his readiness to challenge misfortune." | Nachlass, KSA 12:10[118] | 1887 | Higher man defined |
| "All credibility, all good conscience, all evidence of truth come only from the senses." | Beyond Good and Evil, §134 | 1886 | Empirical basis |
| "Objection, evasion, joyous distrust, and love of irony are signs of health; everything absolute belongs to pathology." | Beyond Good and Evil, §154 | 1886 | Health as irony |
| "Around the hero everything becomes a tragedy; around the demigod everything becomes a satyr play; and around God everything becomes - what? Perhaps a 'world'?" | Beyond Good and Evil, §150 | 1886 | Hierarchy of genres |
| "The man of knowledge must be able not only to love his enemies but also to hate his friends." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Intellectual courage toward friends |
| "The irrationality of a thing is no argument against its existence, rather a condition of it." | Human, All Too Human, §515 | 1878 | Irrationality and existence |
| "Thoughts that come with dove's footsteps guide the world." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "The Stillest Hour" | 1884 | Quiet power |
| "In heaven all the interesting people are missing." | Letter to Peter Gast (attributed) | undated | Wit about afterlife |
| "I could only believe in a God who would know how to dance." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On Reading and Writing" | 1883 | Repeated dancing god |
| "My humanity is a constant self-overcoming." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §8 | 1888 | Humanity as process |
| "Pity in a man of knowledge seems almost laughable, like delicate hands on a Cyclops." | Beyond Good and Evil, §171 | 1886 | Pity unfitting the strong |
| "The good four. Honest with ourselves and with whatever is friend to us; courageous toward the enemy; generous toward the vanquished; polite - always: that is how the four cardinal virtues want us." | The Dawn, §556 | 1881 | Personal ethics rewritten |
| "The problem I thus pose is not what shall succeed mankind in the sequence of living beings (man is an end), but what type of man shall be bred, shall be willed, for being higher in value." | The Antichrist, §3 | 1888 | Breeding the higher type |
| "Mankind surely does not represent an evolution toward a better or stronger or higher level, as progress is now understood. This 'progress' is merely a modern idea, which is to say, a false idea." | The Antichrist, §4 | 1888 | Against progress |
| "The whole of the West no longer possesses the instincts out of which institutions grow, out of which a future grows." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §39 | 1888 | Western institutional decay |
| "The concept 'God' has hitherto been the greatest objection to existence." | Twilight of the Idols, "The Four Great Errors" §8 | 1888 | God as anti-existential |
| "The error of confusing cause and consequence... There is no more dangerous error than that of mistaking the consequence for the cause: I call it reason's intrinsic form of corruption." | Twilight of the Idols, "The Four Great Errors" §1 | 1888 | Against causal confusion |
| "I call an animal, a species, an individual degenerate when it loses its instincts, when it chooses, when it prefers what is harmful to it." | The Antichrist, §6 | 1888 | Degeneration defined |
| "Has a woman who knew she was well-dressed ever caught cold? I suppose, even were she hardly dressed at all." | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §47 | 1888 | Wit on vanity and health |

## Stories They Tell

| Story | Source | Date | Retold? |
|---|---|---|---|
| The death of his father at age 4: "In June 1849 my beloved father suddenly became mentally ill... on July 30th our dear father fell asleep... I dreamed that I heard organ-playing in the church as at a funeral. Suddenly a grave opened and my father in his shroud arose from it." | Autobiographical sketch (age 14) | 1858 | Yes - referenced throughout life, the foundational loss |
| Finding Schopenhauer's World as Will and Representation in a second-hand bookshop: "I don't know what demon whispered to me 'Take this book home.' I took it home and threw myself on the sofa with my new treasure and began to let that energetic gloomy genius work upon me." | Letter to Carl von Gersdorff | late 1865 | Yes - told to multiple correspondents as intellectual awakening |
| First meeting with Wagner at the Brockhaus home, November 1868: "Before and after dinner Wagner played all the important parts of Die Meistersinger... He is indeed a fabulously lively and spirited man, who speaks very fast, is very witty, and makes a private gathering of this kind quite delightful." | Letter to Erwin Rohde | November 9, 1868 | Yes - told as the beginning of his greatest friendship |
| Being appointed professor at Basel at age 24 without completing his doctorate: "Even Ritschl could not refrain from uttering a cry of astonishment. 'Such a thing has never happened before!' he said." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §9 | 1888/1869 | Yes - used as evidence of his exceptional status |
| Military service and chest injury: "I was a bombardier in the second mounted division of the Fourth Horse Artillery... I sprang too eagerly into the saddle and struck my chest against the pommel with great force." | Letters, 1868 | 1867-68 | Told as factual, referenced when discussing his health origins |
| The Franco-Prussian War ambulance service (1870): contracting dysentery and diphtheria while tending wounded soldiers: "I have since had a melancholy succession of experiences... gradually a sense of deep discouragement came over me." | Letter to Carl von Gersdorff | November 7, 1870 | Referenced when discussing his illness trajectory |
| Visits to Tribschen (Wagner's villa on Lake Lucerne): "Those days near Tribschen - days of trust, of cheerfulness, of sublime incidents - of profound moments... I would not banish them from my life at any price." | Ecce Homo, "The Birth of Tragedy" §2 | 1888 (about 1869-72) | Yes - idealized repeatedly in later writings |
| The break with Wagner, crystallized at first Bayreuth Festival (1876): "It was indeed high time to say farewell: soon after I received proof of it. Richard Wagner, seemingly most triumphant, but in truth become a decaying and despairing decadent, suddenly sank down, helpless and broken, before the Christian cross." | Ecce Homo, "Human, All Too Human" §2 | 1888 (about 1876) | Yes - the central narrative of his middle period |
| The moment of conceiving eternal recurrence at Sils-Maria, August 1881: "6,000 feet beyond man and time" - beside a pyramidal rock near Surlej. | Ecce Homo, "Thus Spoke Zarathustra" §1 | 1888 (about Aug 1881) | Yes - mythologized as revelation. Nachlass 11[141] records it: "The beginning of August 1881 in Sils-Maria, 6000 feet above the sea and much higher above all human things!" |
| The genesis of Zarathustra, Part I written in ten days at Rapallo, January 1883: "The whole of Zarathustra I came to me in that sacred hour when Richard Wagner died in Venice." | Ecce Homo, "Thus Spoke Zarathustra" §1 | 1888 | Yes - creative mythos |
| His rejected proposal to Lou Salome (1882): told through letters. "My dear Lou, your idea of reducing philosophical systems to personal acts of their authors is truly a thought from our 'sibling brain.' I myself in Basel taught history of philosophy in this manner." Then: "Lou, if you could see the condition I'm in... I am using up all my remaining self-mastery." | Letters to Lou Salome / Franz Overbeck | 1882 | Referenced obliquely in published works, explicitly in correspondence |
| The Lou-Ree-Nietzsche triangle collapse: his sister's interference, Paul Ree departing with Lou. "This last morsel of life was the hardest I have yet had to chew, and it is still possible that I shall choke on it." | Letter to Franz Overbeck | Christmas 1882 | Yes - told with increasing bitterness through 1883 |
| His isolation narrative - years of nomadic solitude (1880-1888): "My existence is a fearful burden: I should have thrown it off long since, had I not been making the most instructive tests and experiments on intellectual and moral questions in just this condition of suffering and almost absolute renunciation." | Letter to Franz Overbeck | November 14, 1886 | Constant theme in all late letters |
| The illness narrative - migraines, near-blindness, stomach attacks: "I have suffered so much; I have given up so much, that there is no ascetic of any religion who could have any claim on me... My entire Zarathustra is a dithyramb on solitude, or, if I have been understood, on purity." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Told in virtually every letter and in Ecce Homo throughout |
| Almost total blindness during Basel years: forced to resign professorship. "My eyes say no... I must give up reading entirely, and write only in very small doses." | Letters from 1879 | 1879 | Told as liberation from academia |
| Receiving no response to his books: "The most unambiguous signs that the world is not paying the slightest attention to what I write has been given to me often enough." | Ecce Homo, "Human, All Too Human" §1 | 1888 | Constant complaint in correspondence |
| Wagner's death (February 13, 1883): "When I heard of Wagner's death I was overwhelmed... it was hard, very hard, to be for six years the opponent of the man one has most revered." | Letter to Malwida von Meysenbug | February 1883 | Referenced as coinciding with Zarathustra's birth |
| Discovery of Bizet's Carmen as antidote to Wagner (1881): "Yesterday I heard - would you believe it? - Bizet's masterpiece for the twentieth time... this music seems to me perfect." | Letter to Peter Gast | November 1881 | Yes - becomes programmatic in Case of Wagner |
| The horse in Turin (January 3, 1889): collapsing while embracing a horse being beaten. | Reported by multiple witnesses | January 3, 1889 | Nietzsche never told this himself (already collapsed); retold by biographers |
| Writing the madness letters: "I am having all anti-Semites shot" (to Overbeck); "I am every name in history" (to Burckhardt); "I am God, I have made this caricature" (to Burckhardt). | Letters of January 3-7, 1889 | January 1889 | He could not retell; these are the last documents |
| The "Woe! Woe!" moment: discovering Zarathustra Part III writing itself: "If I reckon up the months at Rapallo where this whole tree grew and matured, something astounding happened to me. The entire first part of Zarathustra flashed upon me." | Ecce Homo, "Thus Spoke Zarathustra" §3 | 1888 | Told as creative ecstasy |
| His departure from Basel and academia (June 1879): "With this I take leave of an activity that I cannot carry on any longer." Granted pension of 3000 francs annually. | University Basel records / Letters | 1879 | Told as emancipation, not defeat |
| The stillness of Sils-Maria: "Here I sat, waiting - not for anything - beyond good and evil, fancying now light, now shade, utterly mere game, mere lake, mere noon, mere time without end." | Beyond Good and Evil, "From High Mountains: Aftersong" | 1886 | Poetic self-portrait of the Engadin years |
| Childhood memory - the funeral procession dream: "I heard the dull tones of the church organ. 'What's happening?' A coffin is carried out." He dreamed his brother's death the night before it happened. | Autobiographical sketch | 1858 | Told as establishing pattern of premonition and death |
| Being misidentified as a spy during the Franco-Prussian War and arrested briefly. | Letters | 1870 | Told as absurdist anecdote |
| Composing music for Lou Salome's poem "Hymn to Life": "She thrust upon me a little poem entitled 'Prayer to Life.' It mattered not to what depths I gave to the poem the mood of my muse: perhaps my music will survive me." | Ecce Homo, "Thus Spoke Zarathustra" §1 | 1888 | Told with residual tenderness |
| The Schulpforta years (1858-1864): forming the "Germania" literary club with two friends, submitting original compositions and essays. "We three formed a sort of school within the school." | Autobiographical writings | 1858-64 | Retold in Ecce Homo as foundational |
| Receiving Brandes' lectures news: "This Danish man is clever enough to understand me - I am delighted." | Letter to Peter Gast | April 1888 | First public recognition - told with deep satisfaction |

## Emotional Triggers

| Trigger | Emotion | Evidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Richard Wagner's turn to Christianity (Parsifal) | Betrayal, contempt, grief | "Wagner had become pious... Is it possible that this hatred of life, this poisoning, this rage against all that is earthly, is now to be set to music and thereby made acceptable?" | The Case of Wagner, §3, 1888 |
| Wagner's anti-Semitism and nationalism | Disgust, moral outrage | "Wagner condescended to the Germans - he condescended to becoming a 'Reich German'... I shall never forgive Wagner for having condescended to the Germans." | Ecce Homo, "The Case of Wagner" §2 |
| Christianity / Christian morality | Rage, intellectual fury | "I call Christianity the one great curse, the one great intrinsic depravity... I call it the one immortal blemish upon the human race." | The Antichrist, §62 |
| Being misunderstood / not being read | Deep pain, loneliness | "The worst thing that can happen to a man who cherishes a secret within him is not having found the girl who would die for him, but the friend who would guess it." | Letter to Franz Overbeck, 1882 |
| His sister Elisabeth's anti-Semitism and meddling | Exasperation, contempt | "My sister is really quite impossible. But there we have it: I am the victim of a stupid farce." "Your association with an anti-Semitic chief expresses a foreignness to my whole way of life." | Letter to Elisabeth, December 1887 |
| German nationalism / the Reich | Contempt, intellectual disgust | "I am heartily tired of the German spirit and German culture... Germany, Germany above all - that, I fear, was the end of German philosophy." | Twilight of the Idols; multiple letters |
| Pity / being pitied | Revulsion, anger | "Pity is the practice of nihilism... Pity persuades to nothingness!" "It is disgraceful to be pitied." | The Antichrist §7; letters |
| Physical illness (migraines, blindness, stomach) | Despair alternating with defiance | "This winter was the worst in my life... 118 days of severe attacks." | Letter to Peter Gast, 1880 |
| Solitude / loneliness | Anguish mixed with pride | "My whole Zarathustra is a dithyramb on solitude... In truth a dithyramb on purity... The disgust at mankind, at the 'rabble' has always been my greatest danger." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 |
| Bizet's Carmen / Mediterranean music | Joy, physical elation | "This music seems perfect to me. It approaches lightly, supplely, politely. It is pleasant, it does not sweat." | The Case of Wagner, §1 |
| The Engadin / Sils-Maria landscape | Elation, creative ecstasy | "I know of nothing more suited to my nature than this piece of high land... I could live here for many years." | Letter to Peter Gast, 1881 |
| Nice in winter / the Midi | Health, vitality | "Nice is a wonderful place for me. The light is incomparable." | Letters from Nice, 1884-88 |
| Being dismissed by fellow academics | Wounded pride, defiance | "Not a single one of my friends has taken any notice [of Zarathustra]... This has taught me where I stand - nowhere!" | Letter to Franz Overbeck, 1884 |
| Lou Salome's departure | Devastation, bitterness | "This last morsel of life was the hardest I have yet had to chew, and it is still possible that I shall choke on it... I have suffered from the humiliating memories of this summer as from a bout of madness." | Letter to Franz Overbeck, Christmas 1882 |
| Music (hearing or playing piano) | Tears, transport | "Yesterday evening I was overcome with blissful feelings; the sky still glowed above, and I was playing your piece." | Letter to Peter Gast, 1882 |
| Greek tragedy / Dionysian art | Intellectual rapture | "I was the first to take seriously, for the understanding of the older, the still rich and even overflowing Hellenic instinct, that wonderful phenomenon which bears the name of Dionysus." | Twilight of the Idols, "What I Owe to the Ancients" §4 |
| Democracy / herd mentality | Contempt | "The democratic movement is the inheritance of the Christian movement... an expression of a declining, sinking, tired, sick, weak life." | Beyond Good and Evil §202-203 |
| Anti-Semitism as movement | Furious contempt | "I am just having all anti-Semites shot." | Madness letter to Overbeck, Jan 1889 |
| Being compared to Schopenhauer | Irritation mixed with pride | "I am as far removed from Schopenhauer as I am from... a Buddhist." | Letter to Peter Gast, 1887 |
| Recognition from Brandes | Gratification, relief | "Something very agreeable: Georg Brandes, the well-known Copenhagen critic, has begun a course of lectures on 'The German Philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche.'" | Letter to Peter Gast, February 1888 |
| Peter Gast's music | Hopeful encouragement (often forced) | "Your music gives me feelings... such as no music has ever given me." | Letters to Peter Gast, various |
| Morning walks / landscapes | Euphoria, creative flow | "6 or 7 in the morning is my best time - there are hours of pure happiness." | Letters from Sils-Maria |
| Bad weather / overcast skies | Physical decline, depression | "I need a clear sky above me, otherwise I feel as if I were being pushed underground." | Letters from various locations |
| The memory of his father | Tenderness, loss | "I regard it as a great privilege to have had such a father." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §1 |
| His mother's Christian moralizing | Exasperation, sadness | "Really these dissertations on Christianity... should no longer be directed to my address. My patience won't stand it!" | Letter to Franziska Nietzsche, c. 1884 |
| Stendhal / French culture | Delight, kinship | "Stendhal, one of the most beautiful accidents of my life." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §3 |
| Reading Dostoevsky | Deep recognition | "Dostoevsky, the only psychologist, incidentally, from whom I had something to learn: he ranks among the most beautiful strokes of fortune in my life." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §45 |
| Turin (1888) | Euphoric vitality | "Turin! My dear friend, I congratulate myself - everything succeeds for me here... an incomparable place!" | Letters to Peter Gast, spring-fall 1888 |
| Daybreak / early morning | Joy, clarity | "The mornings are indescribably beautiful - they are my best hours." | Letters |

## Cultural References

| Reference | Type | Context | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heraclitus | Philosopher (pre-Socratic) | "The world forever needs the truth, hence the world forever needs Heraclitus." He identified most with Heraclitus among all Greeks. | Philosophy in the Tragic Age of the Greeks, 1873 |
| Thucydides | Historian | "My recreation, my preference, my cure from all Platonism has always been Thucydides." | Twilight of the Idols, "What I Owe to the Ancients" §2 |
| Sophocles | Tragedian | Model of Apollonian art in tension with Dionysian. "Sophocles and the hero." | The Birth of Tragedy throughout |
| Aeschylus | Tragedian | Representative of the Dionysian sublime: "Aeschylus and the tragic chorus." | The Birth of Tragedy |
| Pindar | Lyric poet | "Become who you are" derives from Pindar's Second Pythian Ode. | The Gay Science §270; letters |
| Plato | Philosopher | "The worst, most durable, and most dangerous of all errors so far was a dogmatist's error - namely, Plato's invention of pure spirit and the Good in itself." | Beyond Good and Evil, Preface |
| Socrates | Philosopher | "I recognized Socrates and Plato to be symptoms of degeneration, tools of the Greek dissolution." Socrates as anti-tragic. | The Birth of Tragedy; Twilight of the Idols, "The Problem of Socrates" |
| Epicurus | Philosopher | "Even today many an educated man thinks that the victory of Christianity over Greek philosophy is a proof of the superior truth of the former - although in this case it was only the coarser and more violent that conquered the more spiritual and delicate." Admired Epicurus. | The Gay Science §45; Nachlass |
| Diogenes | Philosopher (Cynic) | Admired as free spirit. | Various references |
| Empedocles | Pre-Socratic | Admired; planned a drama on Empedocles. | Nachlass, early 1870s |
| Stendhal (Henri Beyle) | Novelist | "One of the most beautiful accidents of my life." Admired his psychological penetration and French clarity. | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §3 |
| Montaigne | Essayist | "That such a man wrote has truly augmented the joy of living on this earth." His model for the philosophical essay. | Schopenhauer as Educator §2 |
| Pascal | Philosopher/theologian | "The admirable logic of Pascal... Pascal, whom I almost love because he has taught me so much." Nietzsche's anti-type whom he respected. | Nachlass; letters |
| La Rochefoucauld | Moralist | Model aphorist. Maxims as precedent for Nietzsche's form. "La Rochefoucauld and Seneca are the two masters I revere most in moralistic reflection." | Nachlass; Human, All Too Human influenced by him |
| Chamfort | Moralist | "A man who knew the human heart" - another French aphorist model. | Human, All Too Human |
| Voltaire | Writer | Dedicated Human, All Too Human to Voltaire's spirit ("free spirit"). | Human, All Too Human, dedication |
| Goethe | Poet/thinker | "Goethe - not a German event, but a European one: a magnificent attempt to overcome the eighteenth century by a return to nature, by an ascending to the naturalness of the Renaissance." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §49 |
| Heine | Poet | "The highest conception of the lyric poet was given to me by Heinrich Heine. I seek in vain in all the realms of millennia for an equally sweet and passionate music." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 |
| Schopenhauer | Philosopher | "I belong to those readers of Schopenhauer who, when they have read one page of him, know with certainty they will read all the pages." Early idol, later adversary. | Schopenhauer as Educator; Ecce Homo |
| Spinoza | Philosopher | "I am utterly amazed, utterly enchanted! I have a precursor, and what a precursor!... His overall tendency is like mine - namely to make knowledge the most powerful affect." | Postcard to Franz Overbeck, July 30, 1881 |
| Emerson | Essayist | "I have never felt so at home in a book... I dare not praise him - he is too close to me." | Letter, early writings |
| Dostoevsky | Novelist | "Dostoevsky, the only psychologist, incidentally, from whom I had something to learn." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §45 |
| Bizet (Carmen) | Composer | "This music is wicked, subtly fatalistic: at the same time it remains popular - it has the refinement of a race, not of an individual." Anti-Wagner. | The Case of Wagner, §1 |
| Wagner (Richard) | Composer | Greatest single cultural reference across all works. From devotion through rupture to polemics. | The Birth of Tragedy; Human, All Too Human; Case of Wagner; Nietzsche contra Wagner |
| Chopin | Composer | Admired for aristocratic refinement. Played frequently. | Letters to Peter Gast |
| Bach | Composer | Deep respect. | Various references |
| Beethoven | Composer | "The music of Beethoven is music about music... the idea of music itself." | Human, All Too Human |
| Schumann | Composer | Early love: "Schumann is my musical Heimat." | Early letters |
| Brahms | Composer | Mixed feelings; respected but saw as epigonal. | Letters |
| Berlioz | Composer | Admired his Dionysian qualities. | Letters |
| Tristan und Isolde | Opera (Wagner) | "Even now I am still in search of a work of equally dangerous fascination... I sought in vain." | Ecce Homo, "The Birth of Tragedy" §2 |
| Parsifal | Opera (Wagner) | "A work of perfidy, of vindictiveness... an outrage against morality." Triggered the final break. | The Case of Wagner; Nietzsche contra Wagner |
| Die Meistersinger | Opera (Wagner) | Admired as Wagner's best dramatic work. | Letters, 1868 |
| The Ring of the Nibelung | Opera cycle (Wagner) | Central reference for Birth of Tragedy period. "The Ring as total artwork." | Birth of Tragedy; letters |
| Carmen (Bizet) | Opera | "I heard Carmen for the twentieth time... How such a work makes one complete!" | The Case of Wagner |
| The Bible / Paul / Gospels | Religious text | Paul as the real founder of Christianity; Jesus as different from Christianity. | The Antichrist; Genealogy of Morals |
| The Laws of Manu | Hindu text | Admired as honest legislation of caste: "An incomparably spiritual and superior work." | The Antichrist, §56-57 |
| Hafiz | Poet (Persian) | Admired as Dionysian poet. | Various |
| Horace | Poet (Roman) | "Up to this moment no poet has given me the same artistic delight as that given from the first by a Horatian ode." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 |
| Petronius (Satyricon) | Roman writer | "That master of presto in invention, ideas, words." | Beyond Good and Evil §28 |
| Sallust | Roman historian | Admired for compact, pointed style. "Sallust's epigrams." | Twilight of the Idols, "What I Owe to the Ancients" §1 |
| Shakespeare | Dramatist | "The finest thing I have to say about Shakespeare is this: he believed in Brutus." Referenced Julius Caesar as model of noble tragedy. | The Gay Science §98 |
| Byron | Poet | "Lord Byron and the extravagance of the individual." | Various |
| Napoleon | Historical figure | "Napoleon, this synthesis of the inhuman and the superhuman." | On the Genealogy of Morals, I §16 |
| Julius Caesar | Historical figure | Admired as supreme example of will to power and clemency combined. "Caesar with the soul of Christ." | Nachlass; letters |
| Alexander the Great | Historical figure | Model of Dionysian abundance. | Birth of Tragedy |
| Frederick the Great | Historical figure | Last German he admired. | Letters |
| Cesare Borgia | Historical figure | "A kind of Superman" - provocative counter-ideal to the saint. | The Antichrist §46; Nachlass |
| Luther | Reformer | "Luther, that calamitous monk who restored the church." Attacked for coarseness and regression. | Beyond Good and Evil §46; Antichrist |
| Schiller | Poet | "Schiller's Moral Trumpet of Sackingen" - gentle contempt for his moralizing. | Beyond Good and Evil §244 |
| Kant | Philosopher | "The great Chinaman of Konigsberg." "A catastrophic spider." Anti-Kant throughout later work. | The Antichrist; Twilight of the Idols |
| Hegel | Philosopher | "A race-corruption of the first order" in his effect on German thought. "A 'great man'? No! merely a great buffoon!" | Nachlass; letters |
| Darwin | Scientist | "The struggle for existence - that is an exception. The general aspect of life is not indigence, famine, but rather riches, profusion, even absurd squandering." Anti-Darwinian. | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §14 |
| Mill (John Stuart) | Philosopher | "I abhor the man's vulgarity when he says: 'What is right for one man is right for another.'" | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §12 |
| Spencer (Herbert) | Philosopher | "Spencer as decadent." | Nachlass |
| Comte (Auguste) | Philosopher | "The idea that the sciences should be organized... a symptom of the same type as Wagner." | Nachlass |
| Leibniz | Philosopher | Respected but criticized for optimism. | Various |
| Descartes | Philosopher | "Descartes, father of rationalism (and hence grandfather of the revolution)." | Beyond Good and Evil |
| Sainte-Beuve | Critic | "That effeminate little man." Contempt for his lack of intellectual courage. | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §3 |
| Flaubert | Novelist | Admired for style: "Flaubert, the greatest novelistic artist." | Letters |
| Balzac | Novelist | Admired. "Balzac's Vautrin: a perfect type of criminal." | Nachlass |
| Baudelaire | Poet | "A kind of Richard Wagner without the music." | Nachlass |
| Turgenev | Novelist | Read and admired. | Letters |
| Maupassant | Writer | Admired: "These Frenchmen of the strong race." | Letters |
| Taine (Hippolyte) | Historian/critic | Admired as scientific historian of culture. | Letters |
| Burckhardt (Jacob) | Historian | "That profoundly meditative Baseler." His model of cultural history. | Letters; Human, All Too Human |
| Dionysos | Mythological figure | Central to entire philosophy - from Birth of Tragedy to Ecce Homo's last line: "Dionysos against the Crucified." | All works |
| Apollo | Mythological figure | The principle of form, individuation, dream - paired with Dionysos in Birth of Tragedy. | The Birth of Tragedy |
| Zarathustra / Zoroaster | Historical/mythological | "Zarathustra created this most calamitous error, morality; consequently, he must also be the first to recognize it." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §3 |
| Jesus / Christ | Religious figure | Distinguished Jesus from Christianity: "In truth, there was only one Christian, and he died on the cross." | The Antichrist §39 |
| Paul (Apostle) | Religious figure | "Paul the apostle... that morbidly lascivious and wicked falsifier... the real author of Christian morality." | The Antichrist §§42-45 |
| Buddha / Siddhartha | Religious figure | Respected: "Buddha is far more positivistic." | The Antichrist §20-23 |

## Disagreement Samples

| Sample | With whom | Context | Source | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "Wagner is merely one of my illnesses... Not that I wish to be ungrateful to this illness. When in this essay I maintain the proposition that Wagner is harmful, I wish no less to maintain for whom he is indispensable - for the philosopher. Others may be able to get along without Wagner; but the philosopher is not free to do without Wagner." | Richard Wagner | Opening and framing of his anti-Wagner polemic | The Case of Wagner, Preface | 1888 |
| "Wagner flatters every nihilistic (Buddhistic) instinct and disguises it in music; he flatters everything Christian, every religious expression of decadence... Wagner is a great corruption of music. He has discovered in it a means of exciting tired nerves." | Richard Wagner | Core charge: Wagner as decadent | The Case of Wagner, §5 | 1888 |
| "I understood Wagner's music as though it were my own: as if with it I first found again what I had lost from youth onward... I now know that since I freed myself from Wagner I was free for the first time in many years." | Richard Wagner | Declaring independence after admitting depth of bond | Nietzsche contra Wagner, "Where I Admired" | 1888 |
| "Has it been observed that music liberates the spirit? Gives wings to thought?... The more one becomes a musician, the more one becomes also a philosopher. The grey sky of abstraction rent as if by lightning; the light strong enough for the filigree of things; the great problems near enough to grasp; the world surveyed as from a mountain. - I have just defined the philosophical pathos. - And unexpectedly answers drop into my lap, a little hail of ice and wisdom, of solved problems..." | Richard Wagner (via Bizet contrast) | Using Bizet to diagnose Wagner's heaviness | The Case of Wagner, §1 | 1888 |
| "Christianity was from the beginning, essentially and fundamentally, life's nausea and disgust with life, merely concealed behind, masked by, dressed up as, faith in 'another' or 'better' life." | Christianity | Direct assault on Christianity's motivational structure | The Birth of Tragedy, "Attempt at Self-Criticism" §5 | 1886 |
| "I regard Christianity as the most fatal seductive lie that has ever existed... The concept 'God' invented as a counter-concept to life... The concept of the 'soul,' the 'spirit,' finally even 'immortal soul,' invented in order to despise the body." | Christianity | Systematic accusation | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §8 | 1888 |
| "Paul simply shifted the centre of gravity of that whole existence after this existence - in the lie of the 'risen' Christ. At bottom he had no use for the life of the Saviour - what he needed was the death on the Cross." | Paul / Christianity | Distinguishing Jesus from Paul's invention | The Antichrist, §42 | 1888 |
| "Kant's Critique of Pure Reason. A catastrophic spider." "Kant's joke: he wanted to prove in a way that would dumbfound the common man that the common man was right." | Immanuel Kant | Dismissing Kant's project as elaborate confirmation of naive belief | Ecce Homo; The Gay Science §193 | 1888; 1882 |
| "Kant, the cunctator of Konigsberg, the great Chinese of Konigsberg." | Immanuel Kant | Kant as procrastinator, as foreigner to life | Nachlass, KSA 13:15[88] | 1888 |
| "I deprecate Schopenhauer's day-by-day philosophy... His fundamental proposition, nihilism, can be refuted simply by the fact that the world goes on." | Arthur Schopenhauer (late) | Breaking from his former master | Nachlass, early 1880s | c. 1881 |
| "The error of Schopenhauer: he was a man of the first rank who was condemned to be a philosopher of the second rank." | Arthur Schopenhauer | Ranking Schopenhauer's thought beneath his person | Nachlass | 1880s |
| "David Strauss, the confessor and the writer. - This renowned... beer-bench classicism... It seems to me that Strauss has never seriously considered what it means to be a follower of Darwin." | David Strauss | First Untimely Meditation - attacking German cultural philistinism | Untimely Meditations I | 1873 |
| "German culture: a contradiction in terms." | German culture broadly | Summary dismissal | Ecce Homo, "The Case of Wagner" §1 | 1888 |
| "One pays heavily for coming to power: power makes stupid... The Germans - once they were called the people of thinkers: do they think at all today?" | German culture | Power and stupidity linked to the Reich | Twilight of the Idols, "What the Germans Lack" §1 | 1888 |
| "The preachers of equality! To me they are tarantulas and secretly vindictive ones... 'Will to equality' - that itself shall henceforth be the name of virtue; and against everything that has power we will raise our clamor!" | Democracy / egalitarianism | Zarathustra's direct attack on equality-advocates | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Tarantulas" | 1884 |
| "One should not wish to enjoy where one does not give joy. And - one should not wish to enjoy!" | Pity / compassion | Anti-eudaemonist position against sympathetic pleasure | Nachlass (attributed) | 1880s |
| "Pity is the most agreeable feeling among those who have little pride and no prospects of great conquests: for them easy prey - and that is what all who suffer are - is enchanting." | Pity | Pity as power-grab of the weak | The Gay Science §13 | 1882 |
| "Against John Stuart Mill: I abhor his vulgarity which says: 'What is right for one man is right for another'; 'Do not unto others that which you would not that they should do unto you.' Such principles would fain establish the whole of human traffic upon mutual services." | John Stuart Mill | Rejecting utilitarian reciprocity as vulgar | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §12 | 1888 |
| "I am frightened by the thought of what unqualified and unsuitable people may invoke my authority one day. Yet that is the torment of every great teacher of mankind: he knows that, given the circumstances and the accidents, he can become a disaster as well as a blessing to mankind." | Future misreaders | Prophetic concern about misuse of his ideas | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Destiny" §1 | 1888 |
| "I want, once and for all, not to know many things. Wisdom sets limits to knowledge too." | Intellectual opponents generally | Defense of selective ignorance | Twilight of the Idols, "Maxims and Arrows" §5 | 1888 |
| "Carlyle: a rhetorician out of necessity, constantly stimulated by the craving for a strong faith and the feeling of his incapacity for it (in that, a typical romantic!)." | Thomas Carlyle | Diagnosing Carlyle's spiritual dishonesty | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §12 | 1888 |
| "George Eliot. - They are rid of the Christian God and now believe all the more firmly that they must cling to Christian morality... In England one must rehabilitate oneself after every little emancipation from theology by showing in a veritably awe-inspiring manner what a moral fanatic one is." | George Eliot | Attacking English secular moralism | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §5 | 1888 |
| "Sainte-Beuve: nothing masculine... always dragged along by circumstance... that effeminate little man." | Sainte-Beuve | Contempt for cowardly criticism | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §3 | 1888 |
| "Renan. - Theology, or the corruption of reason by 'original sin' (Christianity).
