Witness Renan, who, whenever he dares a Yes or No of a more general nature, misses the point with painful regularity." | Ernest Renan | Renan as genteel theological corruption | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §2 | 1888 |
| "Zola: or 'the delight in stinking.'" | Emile Zola | One-line demolition | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §7 | 1888 |
| "Dante: or the hyena who writes poetry in graves." | Dante Alighieri | Brutal dismissal | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §1 | 1888 |
| "Seneca: or the toreador of virtue." | Seneca | Aphoristic compression of contempt | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §1 | 1888 |
| "Rousseau: or the return to nature in impuris naturalibus." | Jean-Jacques Rousseau | Nature-worship as degradation | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §1 | 1888 |
| "Hugo: or the lighthouse on the sea of nonsense." | Victor Hugo | French romanticism dismissed | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §1 | 1888 |
| "Liszt: or the school of running - after women." | Franz Liszt | Character-attack as criticism | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §1 | 1888 |
| "Your letter made me very sad. If you could only feel how insulting your tone of 'sympathy' is to a man like me!" | His sister Elisabeth | Rejecting her patronizing concern | Letter to Elisabeth, 1885 | 1885 |
| "My patience with the German character is wearing thin... A race that produces Bismarck belongs to mediocrity." | Germany (Bismarck specifically) | Anti-national polemic | Letters from Nice | 1887 |

## Opinions on Contemporaries

| Person | What they said | Source | Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| Richard Wagner | "Wagner is admirable and gracious in the invention of the smallest things, inconceivably rich in details that no one else could dream of - he is our greatest miniaturist of music." | The Case of Wagner §7 | 1888 |
| Richard Wagner | "Wagner was a born poisoner." | Nachlass, KSA 13:14[61] | 1888 |
| Richard Wagner | "I loved Wagner and no one else." | Letter to Peter Gast | 1883 |
| Cosima Wagner | "Ariadne... From time to time we practice magic..." (identifying Cosima as Ariadne) | Madness letter to Cosima | January 3, 1889 |
| Franz Overbeck | "My friend Overbeck in Basel is the one person whose judgment and humanity I trust most." | Letter to Peter Gast | 1882 |
| Peter Gast | "My good friend, my maestro Pietro - perhaps the only man living who truly understands me." | Letters to Gast | 1880s |
| Paul Ree | "He is a very thoughtful, very keen mind, a born psychologist... Dr. Ree is one of the bravest and most straightforward people I know." | Letter to Malwida von Meysenbug | 1877 |
| Paul Ree | "Dr. Ree... has always been one of my best readers." (later bitter silence) | Letter | 1876 |
| Lou Salome | "I have found no more gifted or more reflective person... she has an incredibly definite character and knows exactly what she wants." | Letter to Paul Ree | 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "This skinny dirty smelly monkey with her false breasts - a disaster!" (after the break) | Letter to Elisabeth (bitter period) | late 1882 |
| Elisabeth Nietzsche | "My sister, unfortunately, is an incurable invalid with regard to all questions of honor and more subtle questions of morality; and right from the first I have been the victim of her vindictive actions." | Letter to Franz Overbeck | December 1887 |
| Elisabeth Nietzsche | "An unspeakably stupid person, who has chosen precisely the wrong moment to demonstrate her stupidity - I mean her marriage to an anti-Semitic agitator." | Letter to Franz Overbeck | 1885 |
| Bernhard Forster | "This accursed anti-Semite... I could not do anything more opposed to my nature than marry into the anti-Semitic movement." | Letters about his brother-in-law | 1885-86 |
| Georg Brandes | "This Danish man is clever enough to understand me - I am delighted." | Letter to Peter Gast | April 1888 |
| Jacob Burckhardt | "Our great, greatest teacher." | Letters from Basel period | 1870s |
| Erwin Rohde | "Rohde is a true friend - one of the rarest things on earth." (Before cooling.) | Letters | 1870s |
| Hippolyte Taine | "Taine is the first historian who has the courage to see men as they are." | Letters | 1887 |
| Dostoevsky | "Dostoevsky, the only psychologist from whom I had something to learn." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §45 | 1888 |
| Stendhal | "Stendhal, one of the most beautiful accidents of my life - for whatever epoch-making I have encountered has come to me of itself, never through a recommendation." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §3 | 1888 |
| Goethe | "Goethe conceived a strong, highly cultured man, skilled in all bodily accomplishments, who, keeping himself in check and having reverence for himself, dares to afford the whole range and wealth of being natural." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §49 | 1888 |
| Heine | "The highest conception of the lyric poet was given me by Heinrich Heine. I seek in vain in all the realms of millennia for an equally sweet and passionate music." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 |
| Schopenhauer | "Schopenhauer, as a personality, is what gave him his life in my eyes... the first admitted and uncompromising atheist among us Germans." | The Gay Science §99 | 1882 |
| Emerson | "Emerson. - I have never felt so at home in a book, and in my home, as - I may not praise it, it is too close to me." | Nachlass / Letters | 1882 |
| Bismarck | "Bismarck... has ruined the mind of Germany in the service of the 'power of the Reich.'" | Ecce Homo | 1888 |
| Darwin | "Darwin has forgotten the mind (that is English!): the weak have more mind... One must need mind to acquire mind - one loses it when one no longer needs it." | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" §14 | 1888 |
| Kant | "The great Chinaman of Konigsberg was merely a great critic." | Letters | 1880s |
| Spinoza | "I am utterly amazed, utterly enchanted! I have a precursor, and what a precursor!" | Postcard to Overbeck | July 30, 1881 |
| Voltaire | "Voltaire! Humanity! Truth!" (dedicating Human, All Too Human to his spirit) | Human, All Too Human dedication | 1878 |
| Montaigne | "That such a man wrote has truly augmented the joy of living on this earth." | Schopenhauer as Educator §2 | 1874 |
| David Strauss | "This beer-bench Philistine... The Old Believers and the New Faith is the most pitiful book I have read in years." | Untimely Meditations I | 1873 |
| Malwida von Meysenbug | "A noble nature - but one of those people who understand nothing of my real problems." | Letters to Overbeck (about Malwida) | 1880s |
| Wilamowitz | "That young man who attacked my Birth of Tragedy... he is now the most famous philologist in Germany. That says everything about German philology." | Letters | 1880s |
| Ritschl | "Ritschl, who made me a professor... called me the most gifted student he had seen in 39 years of teaching." | Letters; Ecce Homo | 1869/1888 |

---

*Note: All quotes are drawn from standard English translations of Nietzsche's published works (KSA), collected letters (KGB), and the Nachlass fragments. Primary translation sources: Walter Kaufmann, R.J. Hollingdale, and the Stanford Nietzsche Project editions.*
